text: ALMEIRIM, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 4 m. SE of Santarem. Pop. 1,400.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, at the mouth of the Paru.
ALMELOO, a town and district of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, on the Regge, a branch of the Vecht, 24 m. ENE of Deventer. It has some trade in linen and cotton goods. Pop. 4,800.
ALMEN, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Gueldres, arrond. of Zutphen, on the Berkel, 6 m. E of Zutphen. Pop. 2,350.

ALMENARA, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, prov. of Castillon, near the coast, 8 m. N by E of Murviedro.—Also a v. in the prov. of Lerida, 12 m. N of Lerida, on the r. bank of the Negera Ribagorzana. On the 27th of July 1710, the army of Philip, having entered Catalonia, and been repulsed by the allies, were in retreat upon Lerida, when they were attacked at this place and signally defeated by Stanhope.
ALMENDRAL, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, 16 m. S by E of Badajoz.—Also a suburb of Valparaiso.
ALMENDRALEJOS, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Badajoz, 19 m. W by S of Badajoz.
ALMENECHES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, arrond. of Argentan, 18 m. N of Alençon. Pop. 1,005, chiefly linen-weavers.
ALMENGAL, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, between the Fletschhorn mountain and the Saas Rosa river, 21 m. SE of Leuk.
ALMENHAUSEN, a village of Germany, in the principality of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen. Pop. 534.—Also a v. in East Prussia, 15 m. SSE of Konigsberg.
ALMENING, an island of Norway, in the bailiwick of Sodre-Trondheim, near the island of Hitteren. The cathedral of Drontheim is built of stones quarried in this island.
ALMENO-SANTO-SALVATORE, a town of Lombardy, 7 m. NW of Bergamo, near the Brombo river.
ALMER, a parish in Dorsethire, 6 m. SSE of Blandford. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 189.

ALMERIA, a modern prov. of Spain, forming the eastern portion of the kingdom of Granada; bounded on the NE by Murcia; on the SE and S by the Mediterranean; on the W and SW by Granada, from which it is separated by the Sierra Nevada. Its length from N to S is about 90 m, and from E to W 80 m. The surface is covered with lofty mountain-ridges, amongst which occur large and fertile vallies. The coast is rocky on a few points, but elsewhere low and sandy; and forms between Cabo de Gata and Punta Santa Elena a large bay. To the E of the city of A., an extensive flat borders the Mediterranean as far as the Cabo de Gata, distant 18 m. The open tract contiguous to A., rises gradually, and by a series of terraces, towards the N, terminating at the southern base of the Sierra de Alhamilla, which is composed of mica-slate; and the Mediterranean has probably, at some remote epoch, washed the base of this sierra. The principal rivers are the Almansora, the Almeria, and the Adra, all of short course, and discharging themselves into the Mediterranean. The climate on the low grounds and coast is hot; but the snow-covered mountain-summits moderate the temperature in the interior and elevated districts. Earthquakes are frequently experienced, especially at the little fishing v. of Roquetas. The soil is fertile in corn, and produces also cotton and saffron. Citrons, figs, almonds, grapes, pomegranates, and olives, are abundant and of great perfection. Among the mineral productions are copper, lead, iron, antimony, marble, and jasper. Bees and the silk-worm are profitably reared. The principal manufactures are those of soda and saltpetre.-The pop. was estimated in 1836 at 235,000. The chief towns are Almeria, Mujucar, Dalia, and Purchena. The partidos judiciales are Almeria, Berja, Canjayar, Gergal, Huercalovera, Purchena, Sorvas, Velezrubro and Vera; and these are subdivided into 114 pueblos. The city of Almeria, the cap. of the above province, is situated near the mouth of a small river of the same name, at the bottom of the bay of A., in N lat. 36° 51½, W long. 2° 31'. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Granada. It is an agreeable cheerful place. Its port is good, and it conducts a considerable commerce in fruits, barilla, saltpetre, lead, and grain. Pop. 16,000.—This city is the Murges or Portus magnus of the Romans, and the Al Meryah [i. e. 'the Conspicuous'] of the Arabs. While held by the Moors it was a seat of pirates. It was wrested from them by the united efforts of the Spaniards and Genoese on the 16th of October, 1147.
ALMERODE, a town of Hesse Cassel, in the circle of Wetzenhausen, 13 m. ESE of Cassel. Pop. 1,900.
ALMESE, a commune and village of Sardinia, 20 m. S of Suse.
ALMESEGO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Prauly, on the r. bank of the Gorguea, in Slat. 9° 30'.
ALMETEVA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, 34 m. NNW of Burgulma.
ALMINHAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 11 m. NNW of Castello Branco.

ALMIRANTE, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Truxillo, on an affluent of the Moyobamba, in S lat. 6° 45'.—Also a v. of Brazil, in the prov. of Paraiba, on the Aracai river. - Also a river of the United States, rising in Alabama, and flowing into Pensacola bay.

ALMIRANTE BAY, the western part of an extensive bay on the coast of Colombia, between Point Valencia, in N lat. 9° 13′, and Punta Gorda de Tirbi, 14 leagues to the NNW, which is separated into two parts by a chain of kays.

ALMISSA, a town of Dalmatia, situated on a level point of land bathed by the river Cettina on one side, and the Adriatic on the other, 20 m. NW of Macarsca. Pop. 1,200. The territory of A. extends 15 m. along the shore, as far as Brella. It produces excellent wine. A. is called Omish by the Slavonians; and is perhaps the Onæum of ancient geographers.-Fortis.
ALMKERK, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Brabant, arrond. and 12 m. SW of Bois-le-duc. Pop. 1,250.
ALMO, a town of Sweden, in the prov. of Koparberg, 20 m. S by W of Mora.—Also an affluent of the Tiber, below Rome.
ALMODOVAR, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 12 m. S of Ourique. Pop. 2,600.
ALMODOVAR DEL CAMPO, a town and partido of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of Ciudad Real, near the l. bank of the Veja. Pop. 3,000.
ALMODOVAR DEL RIO, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 15 m. W by S of Cordova.

ALMOLONGA, a village of Guatimala, about a league to the SW of Old Guatimala. It contains a splendid church, and a Franciscan convent. It is a disputed point with Spanish historians whether the first Spanish settlement in this country was at this place, or at Tzacualpa, a v. at a short distance; but the Indian inhabitants of A. claim the privileges of nobility as being descended from those Mexicans who accompanied Alvarado at the conquest. Their principal employment is collecting and supplying the capital with fruit and vegetables.
ALMONACID, a river of Spain, in Aragon, rising on the border of the prov. of Terruel, and flowing into the Ebro at Romana. ALM
ALMONACID DE ZORITA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of Guadalaxara, on the l. bank of the Tagus, 34 m. W of Madrid.
ALMONASTER LA REAL, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Huelba, 16 m. SSW of Aracena.

ALMOND, a stream of Mid Lothian, rising in the muir of Shotts, and flowing into the frith of Forth near Cramond. Near Clifton-hall, the Edinburgh and Glasgow Union canal is carried over this river by a noble aqueduct; and within sight of this work, a little lower down, the Edinburgh and Glasgow railroad crosses it by a stupendous viaduct consisting of 36 arches of 50 ft. span, and varying from 60 to 80 ft. in height, which is connected by a lofty earthen embankment with another viaduct of 7 arches of 60 ft. span.—Also a river of Perthshire, rising at the head of Glen Lednock, and falling into the Tay, a little above Perth, after a SE course of 20 m. The Scottish Midland Junction railway crosses this river by a viaduct at a point nearly opposite Scone palace.
ALMOND, a village in Alleghany co., New York, U. S., 14 m. E of Angelica. Pop. 400.

ALMONDBURY, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 30,140 acres. Pop. 37,315, chiefly engaged in the woollen manufacture. The town of A., 13 m. SE of Huddersfield, is supposed by Camden to be the Campedonum of the Romans. The pop. of the town and township, which includes several hamlets, in 1801 was 3,751; in 1841, 8,828.
ALMONDSBURY, a parish in Gloucestershire. Area 6,950 acres. Pop. 1,584. The v. and tything of A., 41 m. S by W of Thornbury, had a pop. in 1841, of 603.
ALMONT, a town of Persia, in Mazanderan, at the foot of the Demawend mountains, 90 m. SW of Farabad.

ALMONTE, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Huelba, 14 m. E by S of Moguer.—Also a small river in Estremadura, rising in the Sierra de Guadelupe, and flowing into the Tagus a little above Gorovillas.

ALMORAH, a city of Northern India, the modern capital of Kumaon, in N lat. 29° 35', E long. 79° 40', 90 m. N by E from Bareilly, and 106 m. NE from Moradabad. It is built on the ridge of a micaceous schist mountain, at an elevation of 5,400 ft. above sea-level; and is backed by an immense snowy range of mountains, higher than the Andes, in which Ramnee, nearly 26,000 ft. in alt., appears due N of A. It is a well-built town, forming a single street nearly a mile in length, and about 50 ft. in width, in which the houses are of stone and slated, and generally of two or three stories in height. "They all stand on a lower story of stone, open to the street, with strong square pillars where are the shops. Above, the buildings are of timber, exactly like those of Chester, in one, or sometimes two very low stories, and surmounted by a sloping roof of heavy grey slate, on which many of the inhabitants pile up their hay, in small stalks, for winter-consumption. The street has a natural pavement of slaty rock which is kept beautifully clean." [Heber.] The original cap. of Kumaon was Chumpawut, but the Gorkhas, entertaining the design of pushing their conquests to the NW, fixed upon A. as their head-quarters. It was taken by the British forces in 1815; and the hon. company have here a garrison of sepoys, two corps of artillery, and two provincial corps of Gorkhas. The lines for the provincial troops are in the valley at the northern foot of the ridge, 2,500 ft. below the town. A. is much resorted to by Europeans for the benefit of their health. It is subject to earthquakes. "Scarcely a year passes without a shake or two." The mountainous unproductive district of A. is separated from Bareilly by the Kumaon hills, and properly speaking is only a subdivision of the Kumaon district.
ALMORADI, a town of Spain, in Valencia, 10 m. SW of Elche.

ALMORAIMA, a vast forest district of Spain, in Andalusia, between San Roque and Ximena, intersected by the Guadarauque river. It consists principally of cork, oak, and ilex; but in the sotos or marshy parts of it, the ash, willow, and other trees grow luxuriantly. Immense herds of swine and cattle are pastured in it; and it abounds in deer, wild boars, and wolves.
ALMSFORD, or Ansford, a parish of Somersetshire, 1 m. Nof Castlecarey. Area 920 acres. Pop. 293.
ALMSTADT, a town of Sweden, in Smaaland, 4 m. E of Christianstadt.
ALMSTEDT, a village and district of Hanover, in the principality of Hildesheim.
ALMUDEBAR, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. of Huesca, 22 m. NNE of Saragossa.

ALMUNEÇAR, a port of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 32 m. S of Granada, in N lat. 36° 45', W long. 3° 45'. A considerable quantity of cotton and sugar is raised in the neighbourhood; but the only commerce is that of a few coasting-vessels. Pop. 2,000.
ALMUNIA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, on the frontier of Aragon, 18 m. NE of Calatayud, near the confluence of the Grio and the Xiloca. Pop. 3,000.
ALMURADIEL, a town of Spain, in New Castile, 30 m. SE of Almagro, on the road across the Sierra Morena between Madrid and Cordova.
ALMUZAFES, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, on the Albufera lake, 15 m. S of Valencia.
ALNA, a township in Lincoln co., state of Maine, U. S., 10 m. N of Wiscasset. Pop. 990.
ALNAMBO, a town of Africa, on the coast of Zanguebar, at the mouth of the Jubo.

ALNE, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW by S of Easingwold. Area 10,250 acres. Pop. 1,703.—Also a p. in the co. of Warwick, 3 m. NE by E of Alcester. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 404.—Also a stream in the same co. which rises near Beardmore, and joins the Arrow at Alcester. See also ALLEN.
ALNESS, a parish in the shire of Ross stretching along the Ness and the Alt rivers. Pop. in 1801, 1,072; in 1841, 1,269, of whom 202 were in the v. of A.
ALNHAM, a parish of Northumberland. Area 16,360 acres. Pop. 256. The township and v. of A. is 12 m. W of Alnwick. Pop. 141.
ALNO, an island of Sweden, in the gulf of Bothnia, off the coast of Hernösand. Pop. 700.

ALNWICK, a parish and borough of Northumberland. The parish, which is in the east division of Coquetdale ward, and comprises six townships, has an area of 16,250 acres. Pop. in 1801, 4,719; in 1841, 6.626.—The town stands on a declivity near the Alne river, which is navigable up to the town by small vessels, 34 m. N by W, by coach-road, from Newcastle, and near the Berwick and Newcastle railway by which it is 314 m. from Berwick, and 384 m. from Newcastle. The pop. of the township, including that on the S side of the river, was 5,443 in 1841; houses 836. The town is well-built, and has a considerable local trade as the principal market of a wealthy agricultural district .- A castle, on the S side of the Alne, and closely adjoining to the town, a seat of the duke of Northumberland, is one of the finest baronial residences in the kingdom.
ALONDI, a small island of Africa, in the bay of Zeyla, off the coast of Adel, in N lat. 11° 50'.
ALONG, a town in the peninsula of Malacca, 30 m. NNW of Ligor.

ALONIA, or Aloni, an island of Asiatic Turkey, in the sea of Marmora, lying to the S of Afzia, and I ALP W of Cyzica. It is included in the sanjak of Biga, and is inhabited chiefly by Greeks, who cultivate corn, cotton, and vines.

ALONI'STENA, a village of the Morea, in the district and 8 hours from the town of Karitena, finely situated on either side of a ravine, at the foot of one of the highest summits of the Mænalian ridge. It probably marks the site of the ancient Helisson.—Leake.
ALOPEKI, a town of the Morea, on the l. side of the Dogano, 32 m. S of Vostitza.
ALOR, an island in the gulf of Bothnia, off the coast of Finland, in N lat. 63° 35'.

ALORA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 20 m. WNW of Malaga. It is seated on the steep declivity of a mountain, overlooking a rich vale, through which the Guadaljore meanders amidst orange and lemon groves. Pop. 4,000. It was a municipal town—Iluro—under the Romans.

ALORE, a ruined town, now a hamlet, on the banks of the Indus, 4 m. SSE of Roree in Sinde, of which it was once the cap. A low bridge of three arches, named 'the bund of A.,' thrown across a deserted bed of a branch of the Indus, alone remains of all its former greatness.—Burnes.

ALORIE, a large city of Yarriba in Western Africa, 3 days' journey SW of Katunga, the existence of which was reported to the Landers. It was represented to them as being of recent origin, but as already two days' journey in circuit, and as being by far the largest and most flourishing city in Yarriba. It is said to be governed by twelve rulers, each of a different nation, and all of equal power.

ALOS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ariège, arrond. of Saint-Girons, on the Souladet. Pop. 1,005. There is an iron-forge here.— Also a v. of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, arrond. of Mauleon. ALOST, Aalst, or Aelst, a city of Belgium, in the prov. of East Flanders, arrond. of Termonde, on the Dender river, 9 m. S of Dendermonde; 15 m. WNW of Brussels; 30 m. S of Antwerp; and 3 m. S of the Audegham station on the railroad from Ghent to Mechlin; in N lat. 50° 55', E long. 4° 1'. Pop. 15,000. The river is navigable up to the town, which conducts a considerable commerce in corn, flax, hops, and oil of colza. Its manufactures consist of soap, hats, lace, pins, thread, pipes, snuff, and leather .- A. was originally founded by the Goths, and known in history so early as 870. In 1360 it was destroyed by fire. It 1667 it was taken by Marshal Turenne.
ALOUPKA. See ALUPKA.
ALOWERE-EL-KEBIR, a high ridge of hills in the Tibbu country in Africa, under the parallel of 24° N. There is a lower range to the W called Alo- WERE SEGHRIR.
ALOXE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, arrond. and 3 m. NW of Beaune. Pop. 264. It is celebrated for its red wine.
ALOYSTHAL, a village of Moravia, in the circle of Olmutz, 12 m. NNE of Schönberg. Iron is wrought in the neighbourhood.

ALP, a generic name given in geographical nomenclature to the different mountain-ranges which form the great system of the Alps. In Switzerland, the term is applied to those pasturages which are found on the most elevated terraces or plateaus: as the Blumlis-Alp, the Ober-Alp, the Unter-Alp, and which, of course, lie under the line of perpetual snow; and the name is derived by some from the Celtic alp, ailp, or alb, signifying a verdant height or green mountain; while others regard it as a corruption of the Latin albus, or the Greek aloos, white, and as having been originally given to mountains whose tops soared above the line of perpetual snow. Cluvier says the Celts called all high mountains alpes or albe; and, in the Gaelic, ailp signifies generally a huge mass, or lump. In Tuscany the term is applied to the outlying masses of the Apennines; and a few rocky hills in the neighbourhood of the Rhone near Beaucaise, are called Alpines. Geographers are accustomed to apply it to any high mountain: and the term alpine is synonymous with mountainous, or mountain-haunting. M
ALPAMARCA, a mining village of Peru, on the eastern descent of the Cordilleras, near the extreme sources of the Amazon, 6 leagues from Banos. There is a silver-mine here.
ALPAR, a town of Hungary, on the r. bank of the Thein, 40 m. N of Szegedin.
ALPEDRINHA, a town of Portugal, in Beira, 19 m. N of Castello Branco.
ALPEDRIZ, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 10 m. SW of Leiria.
ALPEN, a town of Prussia, in the gov. of Cleves, 8 m. SW of Wesel.

ALPES (Basses), or Low Alps, a department of France, consisting of a portion of the ancient Provence, and of the valley of Barcelonette, on the SE frontier, between the parallels of 43° 35' and 44° 40' N, bounded on the N by the dep. of Hautes Alpes or the Upper Alps; on the E by the Piedmontese Alps, by which it is separated from the Sardinian states; on the S by the dep. of Var; on the W by that of Vaucluse; and on the NW by Drôme. Its length from NE to SW is 96 m.; and its medium breadth 50 m. Its superficial area is estimated at 682,643 hectares, or 1,686,810 Eng. acres; an area exceeding that of Lincolnshire by 20,000 acres. It stretches along the western declivity of the Alps, and is nearly throughout its whole extent within the basin of the Durance. The mountains of Lure and Aiguines, branches of the Alpine chain, whose minor ramifications pervade its entire surface, divide it into two districts,—the southern and the northern; but it is characterised throughout its whole extent by a mingling of sterility and cultivation, - districts of the utmost wildness, or clothed in the rude grandeur of Alpine regions, being intermingled with landscapes of great softness and beauty, enamelled with all the luxuriance of a southern soil. The culminating points are the Grand Rubren, 10,983 ft.; the Grand Bérard, in N lat. 44° 27', 10,000 ft.; and Mont Pousène, in N lat. 44° 28', 9,524 ft. The high lands are clothed with excellent pasturage, and higher still with forests of great extent. The valley of the Ubaye or of Barcelonette, on the NE extremity, rivals in magnificence of scenery the finest of the vallies of the Central Alps. —The principal and only navigable river which the dep. possesses, is the Durance, which rises on the eastern frontier of the dep. of the Upper Alps, near Mont Genèvre, and after skirting the SE boundary of the dep. of the Lower Alps, intersects this dep. in a S by W course, and discharges itself into the Rhone near Avignon. The Buech, its principal tributary, enters the dep. a little above its confluence with the Durance, on the r. bank. The other tributaries of the Durance are the Ubaye, the Sasse, the Bleone, which drains the central districts of the dep., the Asse, and the Verdon. All these rivers join the Durance on the L bank; and the Verdon, the most considerable in length, skirts the southern frontier in the lower part of its course. Some of these rivers, by the impetuosity of their descent during inundations, are often productive of much damage to the districts through which they take their course. The Var, with its affluent the Colon, flows in an easterly direction through the SE extremity of the dep. Of the numerous lakes or tarns which this dep. contains, that of Allos—the circumference of which is about 4 m.—is the largest. It is situated at an alt. of 7,300 ft. above sea-level. There are warm mineral springs at Colmar, Digree, and Greoux, and a salt spring at Castellane.—The climate combines all the varieties incident to the southern position and mountainous character of the dep. The air is keen, pure, and salubrious; but subject to great variations of temperature. The higher regions, during a great part of the year, are covered with snow .- The soil, with the ex- ALP ception of that of the plains, is generally sterile and unproductive. The extent of arable land scarcely amounts to one-fourth of the superficies of the dep.; meadow-land occupies a 39th part; and vine-land only a 50th; nearly a 6th is covered with wood; while uncultivated tracts of heath and barren mountains occupy an extent twice as great as that of all the arable district. The principal productions of the soil are grain—of which about 2,400,000 bushels are annually raised, -potatoes, of which the amount grown in 1835 was 721,336 hectolitres, and wine. Oranges, almonds, olives, figs, and grapes, are cultivated in some of the southern districts; and chestnuts, nuts, plums-which in a dried state form an important article of commerce—and the mulberry plant are abundant. The riches of the forests consist in their oaks, beeches, firs, pines, and larches. Some cantons produce excellent truffles. The flora of the dep. is extremely rich. Its mineral wealth is considerable. The existence of gold mines is still hypothetical; but silver has been worked, besides lead, iron, copper, barytes, and bismuth. Rock crystal and jasper are found in considerable quantities, and coal, gypsum, sulphur, potter's clay, fuller's earth, marble, and granite are quarried. The wolf is still common; and the chamois and marmot are hunted by the mountaineers. Game is plentiful; and the larger birds of prey are numerous.—The arts of agriculture have hitherto made little progress in this dep. The principal industrial occupations of its inhabitants are the culture of the olive, and the rearing of bees and silk worms. In 1835, 3,157 kilogrammes, or 6,945 lbs. of raw silk were produced in the dep. The breed of horses is small, but highly esteemed for strength and activity. The mules are excellent. The cattle are also esteemed, and the breeds of sheep have been much improved by the introduction of the Merino. The efforts of manufactural industry are almost entirely confined in this dep. to the supply of its local wants. Some districts possess manufactories of skins, common cutlery, delft-ware, ropes, common cloths, hats, silk, oil, paper, distilleries of brandy, and tanneries, &c.; and in 1834 the dep. contained 15 forges and furnaces, 333 manufactories, and 519 mills. The exports chiefly consist of cattle, honey, wax, dried fruits, wine, and distilled waters.—The dep. is divided into 5 arrondissements, of which the names and chief towns are Digne, Barcelonette, Castellane, Forcalquier, and Sisteron. These are subdivided into 30 cantons and 257 communes. The cap. is Digne. The dep. belongs to the eighth military division, that of Marseilles; and is within the jurisdiction of the royal court of Aix. It forms the dio. of the bishop of Digne. It returned two deputies previous to the late revolution, who were chosen by 479 electors in 1834. —The pop. in 1801 was 133,966; in 1821, 149,340; in 1831, 155,895; in 1836, 159,045, of whom 22,212 were collected in towns of a pop. of 3,000 and upwards, viz., Digne, Forcalquier, Manosque, Sisteron, and Valensolle. In 1841, the pop. was returned at 156,055. In 1839 there existed 295 boys' schools with 7,238 pupils, and 74 girls' schools with 1,478 pupils. These returns appear in small proportion to the pop. In 1846, the proportion who could read and write in the pop. of this dep. were returned at 56.74 in every 100,—a proportion which ranked this dep. in respect of education 35th in the 86 dep. of France.

ALPES (HAUTES), or High Alps, a department in the SE of France, consisting of the SE district of ancient Dauphiné, and a small part of Provence, is situated between the parallels of 44° 6' and 45° 8' N, on the frontiers of Piedmont, from which it is separated on the E by the lofty mountain-chain from which it takes its name; S W and N it is bound- Minish A ·MELS Raab OHolla H dinger .
