M. ed consecutively by the dep. of Basses Alpes, of the Drôme, and of the Isère. It length from NE to SW is 92 m.; its medium breadth 30 m. Its superficies is estimated at 553,264 hectares, or 1,367,115 acres. The entire surface of the dep.—the general inclination of which is towards the Rhone or the SW-is covered with mountain-ranges, forming some of the loftiest ramifications of the Alpine chain. Of these ranges the most important is that consisting of the mountains of Pelvoux, Olan, Chaillol, Le Vieux, Oubioul, Tous, Buc, Ventoux, and Lure, which separates the basin of the Durance from that of the Isère, and divides the dep. into the two natural divisions of the basin of the Isère on the N, and that of the Durance on the S. The Romanche and Drac, which after their junction fall into the Isère in the dep. to which that river gives its name, water the former of these natural divisions; the other is intersected from NE to SW by the Durance, which receives on the r. the Claret, the Guisance, the Gironde, the Alp-Martin, the Biouse, the Vence, the Luie, and the Buech, which latter river waters the SW district of the dep.; and on the l. bank the Servières, the Guil, the Crévoux, and the Vachère. The principal mountain-summits are the Pic des Ecrins or des Oursines, in N lat. 44° 56', E long. 1° 40', the alt. of which is said to exceed 14,000 ft.; the Merdje, in N lat. 45°, E long. 1° 38′, 13,704 ft.; Mont Viso, in N lat. 44° 40', E long. 2° 25', 12,688 ft.; and the Roche- Brune, in N lat. 44° 40′, E long. 2° 7′, 10,905 ft. Of 36 lakes or tarns which the dep. contains, the mountain lakes of Cristaux, Montier, Leirien, and Orciers are the principal. There are saline springs in some districts. The dep., in all its physical aspects, is characterized by extreme variety. The mountain - regions of the south present extensive weather-rifted surfaces, totally devoid of vegetation; while those towards the N are covered with wood to a great height. The lower elevations of the Alpine groups are generally clothed to the summit with pasturage or copse-wood; and even, in fine exposures, admit of the culture of the vine. The extent of arable land which the dep. comprises scarcely amounts to two-elevenths of its entire superficies. The meadowlands, especially those of the Alpine districts, are extremely rich, and occupy about one-fourth of the extent of the arable land. About one-third of the surface is covered with wood; while the remaining portion, amounting to two-fifths of the whole superficies, consists of tracts of heathy pasture-land, and barren wastes. The marshy districts afford excellent turf. The Alpine region presents the three distinet vegetable zones of Lapland, and Central and Southern Europe. The agriculture of this dep. has hitherto made little advancement, except perhaps in its system of irrigation. - The climate is pure and salubrious, but variable in temperature. It is severe and rude in winter; but excessively hot-especially in the vallies-in summer. The autumnal season, the pleasantest in this region, is often prolonged till December. During the long winters to which the higher districts are subjected, the inhabitants are cut off from all intercourse with the neighbouring districts; and the higher summits of the dep. are covered more than half the year with snow. Hail storms are of frequent occurrence, and render the results of agriculture very precarious. The bise or N wind prevails here, and from the excessive cold acquired by it in its passage over the glaciers, is often productive of much injury to vegetation. The W wind-called from its violence le gros vent-generally occurs after rains. The S wind prevails-often in rapid alternation with the N wind-in winter, spring, and autumn; and the E wind is often pernicious to vegetation during the early season of the year. The principal - ALP productions of the soil are grain, of which rye is the staple, hemp, lint, rape, madder in small quantities, and potatoes. The quantity of its productions in grain, in 1835, was estimated at 805,624 hectolitres, [1 hect. = 2.8379 Winchester bushels,] and of potatoes, 522,880 hectolitres. Fruit trees of various kinds are abundant. The prevailing species of forest-trees are larch, fir, ash, lime, chestnut, and maple. The inhabitants rear few horses; mules and asses being better adapted to the mountainous character of the dep. The breeds of cattle are of the smaller species; the sheep are large, and their wool is of excellent quality. The number of sheep reared is estimated at 271,000; of cattle, 33,000; of goats, 18,000; and of pigs, 10,000; besides 150,000 sheep belonging to neighbouring districts, which are annually brought to the mountain pasturages in this dep. The goats of the Alps are said to resemble those of Cashmere, in the length and softness of their hair. Bears, wolves, lynxes, deer, and marmots, inhabit the mountain-forests; and birds of prey-of which the eagle is the most formidable and destructive—are very numerous. Game of all kinds is abundant; and the lakes and rivers afford excellent fish.—The principal mineral productions of the dep. are gold, silver, copper, lead, zinc, iron, antimony, asbestos, gypsum, marble, rock-crystal, slate, alabaster, sulphur, freestone, chalk, granite, porphyry, lithographic stones, and coal.—The manufactures and trade of the dep. are almost exclusively limited to the supply of local wants, and consist in fabrics of linen, cotton, silk, and wool, paper, thread, and ironmongery. The dep. contained, in 1838, 36 forges and blast-furnaces, 467 mills, and 127 manufacturing establishments. Its chief foreign commerce consists in furs, which it exports in considerable quantities to Lyons, in nut oil, timber, wool, cheese, and cattle.—The dep. is divided into the three arrondissements of Gap, Embrun, and Briançon; and subdivided into 24 cantons, containing 189 communes or parishes. Its principal town is Gap. It belongs to the 7th military division; and is under the jurisdiction of the royal court of Grenoble. It forms the dio. of the bishop of Gap, a suffragan of the archbishopric of Aix.—Previous to the late revolution, it elected two deputies, who were chosen by 371 electors in 1834.—The pop. of the dep. in 1801 was 112,500; in 1821, 121,418; in 1841, 132,584; of whom only about 15,000 were collected in towns whose pop. exceeded 3,000 souls. Upwards of 4,000 of the inhabitants emigrate periodically towards the end of autumn to the surrounding departments, and spend the winter in giving instruction in the various handicrafts and occupations to which they are addicted. In 1839 there were 270 primary schools in the dep. attended by 13,000 pupils. The proportion in every 100 of the pop. who could read and write in 1846 was returned at 77.37, a proportion which places this dep. 15th in rank in educational respects.
ALPH, a town of Central Africa, in Bornou, in N lat. 11° 30′, S of Lake Tchad, and W of the Shary.
ALPHAMSTONE, a parish in Essex, 5 m. NE of Halsted. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 314.
ALPHAO, or Alpalhao, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 15 m. NW of Portalegre.

ALPHEIUS, a river of Greece, the largest in the Morea, called by the modern Greeks Trano Potamo, or 'the Long river.' The Upper Alpheïus, usually called the river of Karitena, rises on the eastern border of Arcadia, in the vicinity of Vervena near Mount Málevo, and flows in a SW course past Asea, to a little above its junction with the Karaion descending in a NNW course from the extreme southern mountain-border of Arcadia. Both above and below Asea, the A. sinks into the earth several times, reappearing again after having run some distance, thus authenticating ancient tradition. It then turns NW, and passing to the W of Megalopolis receives the Helisson on its r. bank, and at a short distance to the N of Karitena, the Gortynius or Gortyna, a rapid mountain-stream, bearing the modern name of Kachikolo or Atchikolo. Pursuing a NW course between Karitena and Andrizena, a distance of 10 m., it flows through a deep, narrow, rocky ravine, in some places "below such tremendous precipices as to be wholly invisible" [Gell], and descends into a lower plain, "a broad uneven valley" [Anderson], in which, after turning W and passing Agios Joannes on the site of the ancient Heræa, it is joined on the r. by the Ladon, which Anderson, who saw it in June, says is the larger of the two, and enters the A. "with a narrow but rapid, deep, and turbid stream" [Leake], and a little lower down by the Erymanthus, also on the r. bank, and by the Diagon on the opposite bank. From its junction with the Ladon it assumes the modern name of the Ruféa or Rufía. Throughout the remainder of its course, as far as Olympia, where it receives the Claudeus, "descending from a retired pastoral dell to the N" [Mure], it separates Triphylla from Elis; but after receiving the Selinus on the l. bank, it pursues a meandering course from NW to SW, through a low rich plain, from half-amile to a mile in breadth, and falls into the Cyparissian gulf. Forming the grand drain of the plain of Megalopolis, and a great proportion of the interior districts of the Morea, the A. frequently occasions extensive and rapid inundations; and as it overflows the plain at its mouth every year, the soil has here risen by constant deposits of alluvium, 10 or 15 ft. above its original level. Leake found it, immediately above its mouth, in the end of February, a "full and rapid but turbid stream;" and Anderson, who saw it so late in the season as June, calls it "a rapid muddy stream." Mure, who saw it in April, describes its waters as of "a yellowish cream colour."-Leake's Travels in the Morea. London, 1830, 3 vols. 8vo.—Gell's Narrative.—Anderson's Observations upon the Peloponnesus. Boston, 1830. — Mure's Journal. Edin., 1842, 2 vols.

ALPHEN, a canton or parish and town of Holland, in the prov. of South Holland, district of La Haye, 9\frac{3}{4} m. E of Leyden. Pop. 2,050. The principal manufacture carried on in the town is that of pottery and pipes.-Pop. of the p. in 1835, 13,117 in 1,982 houses. Area 17,256 bunders, or 43,150 acres, of which, in 1835, 10,528 bunders were under pasture and hay land; 3,392 were arable land; 1,666 were occupied with canals and other waters; 460 were under wood; 438 were reed and rush-land and marshes; 261 were returned as heritable property; 233 as dykes, wastes, and low lands; 145 as orchards; and 113 as gardens. These statistics afford an idea of the division and employment of land generally throughout South Holland. The butter and cheese made in this district are esteemed.—Also a v. in North Brabant, arrond. and 9 m. SE of Breda. Pop. 1,500.—Also a v. in Guelderland, on the Meuse, 14 m. W of Nimeguen.—Also a v. in East Flanders, 41 m. SE of Alost.
ALPHETON, a parish in Suffolk, 7 m. N by E of Sudbury. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 321.
ALPHINGTON, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. S of Exeter. Area 2,720 acres. Pop. 1,286.
ALPHONSE, an island in the Indian ocean, in S lat. 7° 6', E long. 53°, 804 m. N 1/4 NW from Mauritius, and 36 m. to the S of the Amirantes.
ALPINES. See ALP.
ALPINGTON, a parish in Norfolk, 6 m. SE of Norwich. Area 630 acres. Pop. 197.
ALPIRSBACH, a town of Wurtemberg, on the ALP S of Freudenstadt. Pop. 1,600. Silver and cobalt are mined in the neighbourhoood.

ALPNACH, or ALPNACHT, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Underwalden, at the foot of Mount Pilate, near the confluence of the Aa with the lake of Lucerne, or with that arm or reach of it which is known as the lake of A. Pop. 1,400, many of whom are engaged in cutting and dressing timber in the extensive spruce-fir forests which clothe the rugged flanks and deep gorges of the Pilatus. The medium height of this forest is about 2,500 ft.; and a German engineer contrived a wooden trough or slide, about 6 ft. broad, from 3 ft. to 6 ft. deep, and 44,252 ft. or 8 m. 3 furl. in length, by which the trees, when felled and lopped, were slid down into the lake. In the construction of this work, which occupied 160 workmen 18 months, from 25,000 to 35,000 trees were employed; but the cost was only 100,000 francs, or £4,250. It crossed three great ravines, one at the height of 64 ft.; another at the height of 103; and the third, where it ran along the face of a rock, at that of 157. In two places it ran under ground. The medium angle of elevation was 3° 14' 20". At the beginning, the inclination was about 22° 30', or one-fourth of a right angle; in many places it was 20°. After the first 500 ft. it was less deep, and often considerably circuitous, according to the direction which the ruggedness of the ground forced it to take. By this contrivance, a tree 100 ft. long, and 4 ft. in diam. at the lower end, was conveyed the whole length of the trough in six minutes; and the larger the tree, the greater was the velocity of its descent: indeed, a tree must be very large to descend at all in this manner, though Inglis states that a tree 90 ft. long, and 2 ft. in diam.,laid on this slide, would accomplish its journey in 21 minutes. The trees thus brought down were formed into rafts, conducted to Lucerne, and floated down the rapid Reuss, first into the Aar to near Brugg, and then into the Rhine, so as to reach Basle in a few days. From Basle they were navigated down the Rhine in rafts, to Holland; and were afloat in the German ocean in less than a month, from having descended from the side of Pilatus, an inland mountain not less than 1,000 m. distant. This magnificent and ingenious work was completed in 1812, but taken down in 1819, after the demand for timber had so greatly fallen off as to render the working of it unprofitable. In 1833 a cut-road was formed into the centre of the forests of the Pilatus, by which the timber, after being dressed into planks, is conveyed to the lake. The money which was paid the commune of Alpnach for the use of their forests, 6,000 crowns, was employed in building the handsome church which now ornaments their village.—Professor Playfair's Works, vol. i. Edin., 1822.—Edinburgh Phil. Journ., No. iii., 1820.

THE ALPS. A lofty mountain-chain, or continous succession of mountain-ranges, constituting, in a crescent-like form, the central and most elevated portion of the great mountain-system which intersects the entire extent of Europe from Gibraltar to the straits of Constantinople, and from the southern extremity of the Italian peninsula to the Baltic: traversing Provence and Dauphiné in France, all Savoy, a great part of Piedmont, the Milanese, all Switzerland, the Tyrol, Salzburg, Carinthia, Carniola, Styria, Croatia, Slavonia, and the southern part of Bavaria, of Suabia, and of Austria. This chain, notwithstanding its vast extent of more than 1,100 m., forms but a moiety of that continuous succession of highlands or uplands by which, from the straits of Gibraltar to the Bosphorus of Constantinople, all the tributary streams of the Mediterranean are in Europe separated from those which, by opposite slopes, run to the Atlantic ocean, and the German and the Euxine seas. The system of the Alps, in respect both of its mass and its elevation, holds the first rank amongst the different mountain-chains which rise on the surface of Continental Europe. From the knot or culminating point in which the St. Gothard, the Vogelsberg, the Bernardine, the Splugen, and the Septimer-the group known to the ancients under the name of Mons Adula -meet as in a common centre, diverge branches by which a connexion is established with the Apennines, the Pyrenees, the Vosges, the Hartz, the Sudetes, the Carpathians, and the Balkan. The Alpine chain presents, in its general aspect, a continuous line of snowcapped mountain-groups, varying in breadth, and in the length and direction of their ramifications, and graduating in altitude from the average height of 7,700 to 15,732 ft. [Humboldt.] It stretches through 12 Kinzig, in a deep valley of the Schwarzwalde, 7 m. | degrees of longitude, or between 6° and 18° E, and has a varying breadth, between the parallels of 44° and 48° N, of from 50 to 150 m. The whole system is supposed to cover an area of 120,000 sq. m., and to be inhabited by a pop. of at least 7,000,000, of whom the greater part are of German origin. Comparative height.] The following rude diagram will serve to convey to the mind of the reader a comparative view of the height of the crest and culminating points of the Alps and the other great mountainchains of our globe. By a train of profound and ingenious reasoning, Baron Humboldt has calculated the mean elevation of all Europe at 670 ft.; and that of the crest of the Alps, represented by the line g, in section E, at 7,700 ft. The highest point in the Alpine chain, Mont Blanc, [15,732 ft.] will then appear as f. A The Pyrenean system, D, has 8,000 ft. average height of crest; and its highest point, the Pic de Nethou, k, is 11,168 ft. The Andes of Quito, represented by C, have their average crest line, l, at 11,800 ft.; and their summit in Chimborazo, c, at 21,420 ft. The Andes of Bolivia, B, have their western crest at 14,900 ft.; their eastern at 15,200 ft.; the summit of their western section, Gualatieri, a, 21,997 ft.; and the two summits of their eastern section, Aconcagua and Illimani, b, respectively 23,200 ft. and 24,200 ft. The crest of the Himalaya system, represented by the line h, has an average of 15,700 ft.; and its two summits, Juwahir, e, and Dawalagiri, d, have an elevation of 25,749 ft. and 28,000 ft. respectively. The principal subdivisions of this stupendous chain are the following: 1st. THE MARITIME OF LIGURIAN ALPS, the Alpes Maritimæ of the Romans, which consist of two sections,—the first extending in a semicircle from the SW extremity of the Alpine chain, to the Col de Lauzania in the SW angle of Piedmont, and forming, to the extent of 45 m., the line of separation between that province and the county of Nice; the second, distinguished as the Upper Maritime Alps, 30 m. in extent, terminating in the lofty summit of Monte Viso, the ancient Vesulus, on the SW frontier of Piedmont. Saussure describes under the name of Maritime Alps the whole of that chain of mountains which runs along the coast of the Mediterranean from Nice to Genoa; but it is more common in the present day to restrict the term as we have done. In the chain now described we have the following altiare: tudes, according to Balbi: Mont Parpaillou, near Barcelonette, . . . 8,931 2d. THE COTTIAN ALPS, which stretch in a triangular form from Monte Viso to Mont Cenis; between the prov. of Turin on one side, and the dep. of Hautes Alpes in France, and Savoy in Sardinia, on the other; and give rise, in their range of about 75 m., to the Durance, the Po, and several minor streams. This chain, the Alpes Cottiæ or Cottianæ of the Romans, is sometimes known by the appellation of MOUNT GENEVRE. Its principal summits are: ALPS. . . . . 3d. The Graian Alps,—a name derived from one of its summits, the Little St. Bernard, which D'Anville identifies with the Alpe Graia of the ancients; but this chain is by German geographers called the Grave Alpen or 'Grey Alps'-extend a distance of 60. m. from Mont Cenis to the Col du Bonhomme, between Savoy on the W, and the provinces of Turin and Aosta on the E; giving rise to the Northern Stura and the Orca rivers in Piedmont, which flow into the Po, and to the Arc and the Isère, which pour their united waters into the Rhone. The most elevated summits in this chain are: Mont Iseran, in N lat. 45° 31', E long. 7° 16', . 13,274 ft. Aiguille de la Sassiere, in N lat. 45° 30', E long. 6° 59′,
Rocca Melone, in N lat. 45° 12′, E long. 7° 4′, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Mont Cenis, in N lat. 45° 14', E long. 6° 45', . . . 11,457 4th. THE PENNINE ALPS, 75 m. in length, run between Savoy and the Valais on one side, and Sardinia on the other; from the Col du Bonhomme to Monte Rosa; and include the three loftiest mountains in Europe, Mont Blanc, in N lat. 45° 50', E long. 6° 51', 15,732 ft.; Monte Rosa, in N lat. 45° 56', E long. 7° 52', 15,150 ft.; and Mont Cervin, in E long. 7° 43', 14,835 ft. Among the other summits are: Le Geant, to the NE of Mont Blanc, . . . 13,800 ft. Aiguille du Midi,
Mont Velan, in N lat. 45° 53′, E long. 7° 15′, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Pic Blanc, E of Monte Rosa, . . . . . 11,190 From the resemblance of Pani and Pennini, some have conjectured that Hannibal may have crossed this part of the Alps, and given it its name; while others trace the appellation to the Celtic pen or ben, a head or elevated summit. 5th. THE LEPONTIAN OF HELVETIAN ALPS take a NE direction, and extending along the southern frontiers of the Grisons, terminate at Monte Bernardino. Among the principal summits in this range are: Mont Furca, 10 m. W of St. Gothard, 14,037 ft. . Monte Leone, or the Simplon, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Pesciora, the highest summit of the St. Gothard group, 10,595 The Moschelhorn or Vogelsberg, in the Rheinwald, 10,870 This is the most visited of all the chains of the Alps, being the most remarkable for magnificent scenery. THE BERNESE ALPS diverge, under the appellation of Luckmanierberg, from this central ridge of the Alps; and running along the southern confines of the canton of Berne, form a junction with the Jura range to the N of the lake of Geneva. This, and the lofty range which forms the southern side of the basin of the Rhone, constituted the Summæ Alpes of the Romans. In this secondary chain of the Alps we have many conspicuous summits, amongst which The Finsteraarhorn, 11 m. W of the Grimsel pass, The Eiger, W of the Schreckhorn, . . . . . 13,075 6th. THE RHÆTIAN ALPS, in which the Alpine chain becomes more complicated, commence at Monte Bernardino, extend 225 m. along the frontiers of Switzerland, Italy, and Germany, and terminate at the Dreyhernn-Spitz in the NE extremity of Tyrol. In this chain we have: Mont Julier, to the N of the sources of the Inn, . From Mont Septimer, one of the SW summits of this range, and the northern extremity of the curve of the ridge which terminates at Monte del Oro, in N lat. 46° 25', E long. 9° 42', a branch called the Alpes des GRISONS runs to the NE, and on reaching the Arlebergs, subdivides into two ramifications, one of which, extending in a NW direction, separates the waters of the Ill from those belonging to the adjacent portion of the basin of the Danube, and terminates near the sources of the Naab and Mayn, in the lofty summit of the Fichtelberg; while the other, bending to the E, in the direction of the course of the Inn, terminates at the junction of that river with the Danube. From Monte del Oro, the Rhætian Alps take a NE direction parallel to the course of the Inn in the Upper Engandine. About midway to Gebatsch or the Kænigspitz,-alt. 12,276 ft., one of the principal sources of the Adige,-they send off another branch, which, passing the lofty Ortlerspitz, runs to the E, and terminates on the great bend of the Adige, directing the course of that river to the south. A corresponding ramification encloses the basin of the Adige on the left. At Dreyherrn-Spitz, in N lat. 47° 5', E long. 12° 15', the principal chain of the Alps forms two great ramifications. The first of these, under the name of 7th. THE NORIC ALPS, stretching through Salzburg, Styria, and Upper and Lower Austria, forms the dividing line of the basins of the Salza and the Drave. In this range are: The Gross Glockner, in N lat. 47° 7', E long. 12° 43', 12,776 ft. The Wisbachhorn, in the N of Carinthia, The Winfeld, in Styria, . . . . 8,581 At Schwartzhorn this range divides into two branches, one of which bends to the NE, and terminates on the northern confines of Styria; the other, distinguished by the name of The Styrian Alps, separates the basins of the Muhr and the Drave. The other great bifurcation of the central Alpine ridge bends in a southerly direction until it reaches Monte Croce, in N lat. 46° 35', E long. 13° 2', whence, under the name of 8th. THE CARNIC ALPS, OF BIRNBAUMERWALD OF the Germans, it runs towards the SE, separating the waters of the Gail from those which flow into the gulf of Venice. From the vicinity of the sources of the Save, Isonzo, and Tagliamento, a branch diverging from this range runs between the basins of the Drave and Save, and unites with the Styrian Alps on the confines of the province from which that range derives its name. The leading summits of the Carnic Alps are: La Marmolata, in N lat. 46° 26', E long. 11° 55', . 11,508 ft. Le Grand Nabois, in the NE of Lombardy, 9,593 Cima d'Asta, the source of the Brenta, 9,198 From Terglou, the Carnic Alps are prolonged through Illyria to Mont Kleck, a distance of 120 m., under the name of 9th. THE JULIAN OF PANNONIAN ALPS, which send one branch northwards into Sclavonia, separating the basins of the Save and the Drave; the other, or southern branch,—described by us in a distinct article as THE DINARIC ALPS, -forms a range of bare and rocky mountains, rising almost perpendicularly on the NW shores of the Adriatic, and stretching thence to the confines of Servia and Macedonia; thus constituting the eastern extremity of the Alpine chain, and completing its stupendous range, or linking it to the Slavo-Hellenic mountain-system. In the northern branch of this range we have: The Terglou, in N lat. 46° 22', E long. 13° 51', .- 10,866 ft. The Welikistal, to the SE of the Keppas, 7,330 The Keppas, 10 m. NNE of the Terglou, 6,887 The Leobelberg, 15 m. SE of Klagenfurt, 7,173 ARRENA PARA LA PARA LA LA CALLA LA LA LA LA LA LA LA LA LA LA LA LA ALPS. The course of the Julian Alps is in many cases very sinuous; but it is generally to the SE, and along the shore of the Adriatic. This chain is almost wholly composed of greyish limestone or dolomite, which for the most part belongs to intermediary strata. It is full of abysses, grottoes, hollows, tunnels and subterranean conduits, in which the waters are lost, or reappear after a hidden course. The connexion of the Alps with the Balkan or Hæmus, is made by the Dinaric mountains, which may be regarded as forming the southern branch of the Julian Alps. This range is the Mons Albanus of Ptolemy, and the Mons Albius of Strabo, names evidently derived from the Celtic alp or alb. We seem thus to have reason, both from etymology and the authority of the ancients, to denominate this prolongation of the chain from the source of the Kulpa to that of the Drin near Perserin, the Dinaric Alps. The particular summit, called Dinari, or Dinara, which gives name to the chain, is 10 m. ENE of Krim in Austrian Dalmatia. From Mount Kleck it runs uniformly SE through the Austrian states of Illyria, Croatia, and Dalmatia, and the Turkish provinces of Bosnia and Albania, a space of 120 leagues. This chain generally resembles the Julian range in its calcareous rocks, which are steep, cut by profound gulfs, and full of numerous caverns and subterranean conduits in which the streams are in some instances lost for many leagues. Their summits are absolutely naked; and their sides present precipitous grey limestone rocks, whose bases are washed by the surges of the Adriatic. Such is an outline of the successive ranges of the Alps,—so many continuous mountain-links which mark the division of the waters, and which consequently form the crest of the Alpine system. From this crest-line we see the lands of Central Europe declining on one side towards the Atlantic ocean, the North sea, and the Baltic, and on the other towards the Mediterranean and the Adriatic; but that declension, slow and gentle on the north side, is very rapid on the south side. The St. Gothard, the culminating point of all these chains of the Alps, is 52 leagues of direct distance from the Mediterranean, 75 from the Adriatic, 175 from the Atlantic, 168 from the North sea, and 185 from the Baltic. Snow-line.] The line of permanent snow, which of course descends as the latitude approaches either pole, is in the European Alps between 8,900 and 9,000 ft. In South America it is 18,300 ft. It is to be remarked, that in the Alps the snow-line is between 600 and 700 ft. lower on the northern than on the southern side. It has been alleged that the lower limit of perpetual snow on the Alps is constantly descending; but this fact is by no means wellestablished. Glaciers.] Perpetual snow covers all the summits of the Alps—estimated at the number of 400— which rise above the level of 9,000 ft.; and amassing in vast accumulations in the adjacent vallies, forms the glaciers, which constitute one of the most remarkable features of the Alps, and give rise to some of the largest rivers in Europe. If from an elevated point, such as the Rigi, we contemplate the Alps, it is easy to distinguish the region of cultivation; higher up that of forests; still higher that of meadows; and finally the region of perpetual snow, whose lower limit is a straight line, sensibly horizontal. In certain spots, however, white trains are seen to descend to the plains: these lines, which occupy the bottoms of vallies, gorges, or ravines, are the glaciers. These glaciers-vernacularly in Tyrol called firn or ferner, and in Switzerland gletscher-often present a nearly level surface; but are more generally rent into deep fissures, forming insurmountable barriers to the adventurous tourist. They are composed, not of snow, but of ice in minute fragments, separated from each other by capillary intervals. Lower down these fragments have nearly the size of a nut; but in proportion as they are higher up, they become less; and at the height of 8,000 ft. they are not larger than a pea. The most remarkable of these Alpine reservoirs are the glaciers of Mont Blanc, whose combined area probably amounts to between 90 and 100 sq. m. [Forbes. That on the northern declivity of that mountain, denominated the Mer de Glace, is 15 m. in length; 6 m. at some parts in breadth; and from 80 to upwards of 100 ft. in thickness. The glaciers are almost exclusively confined to the transverse vallies, which, compared with the great longitudinal basins of the Alpine chain, are, with the exception of that of the Adige, of short extent. They are not motionless masses, but are incessantly and with a "rigorously continuous" motion [Forbes], descending by their own weight to the plain; and they descend lower in proportion as the mountains on which they originate are higher, because the supply of snow from their summit is greater in the same proportion. The mean height of the lower extremity of the four lowest glaciers of the Swiss Alps is 3,690 ft. above sea-level. Ebel estimates the entire extent or superficial area of the glaciers of the Alps at 130 sq. leagues, or 1,400 sq. m. Geology.] In their geological features the Alps present a nucleus of granitic structure, intercalated with masses of gneiss, mica slate, and talcose slate. The declivities consist of deposits of the intermediate epoch, containing organic remains, which gradually become scarcer in proportion to the declinature from the central altitude of the formation in which they are imbedded. The calcareous formation characterises all the mountains of medium elevation which form the outer ranges of the Alps, throughout Switzerland, Swabia, Bavaria, Saltzberg, Styria, Carinthia, Croatia, Bosnia, Servia, and Dalmatia, and all the more isolated ridges on the southern declivity of the Alpine chain. These calcareous formations are succeeded, in many districts, by those which belong to the secondary epoch, but always by the arenaceous deposits of the tertiary period when they terminate in sandy plains.—Considerable quantities of the finest marble are found in all parts of the Alpine chain. The primitive and intermediate formations contain mines of copper, iron, lead, and gold: the later, large beds of anthracite and salt. The central portion of the Alps is particularly rich in minerals, of which the most valuable are the syenite and epidote of the Western Alps; the titanium, green tourmalin, ruby, sapphire, and adulaire of the St. Gothard group; and the iron carbonates of Brosso, in the valley of Aosto. Vegetation.] In the plains at the foot of the Alps a very rich and beautiful vegetation prevails, in which we may often distinguish the plants of the higher Alps, which have sprung from seeds conveyed down by the torrents. Ascending 1,600 or 1,700 feet, we find the Primula auricula or bear's ear covering the rocks with its light yellow flowers; the stemless gentian, Gentiana acauhis, whose large petals of ultramarine blue incline towards the earth; the Aconitium napellum or wolf's bane, the Ranunculus aconitifolius, the Trollius Europæus, &c., &c. At the height of 3,300 ft. the soldanelle, Soldanella alpina, frames the melting snows in a violet border. The Crocus vernus is also found in the same locality; and the Rhododendron ferrugineum, and R. hirsutum, adorn the declivities with their red hues, often ornamenting large surfaces. At the height of 6,500 ft, the greater part of the vegetation of the plain has disappeared. In North Switzerland, the vine does not rise higher than about 1,800 ft.; on the S declivity of the Alps, it attains 2,150 ft.; and in some favourable localities, such as Val-Sesia, at the foot of Monte Rosa, it is cultivated even at the height of 3,300 ft. In the north of Switzerland the cereals rise as high as 3,600 ft., but they do not calculate on a sure harvest beyond 2,900 ft. Maize, however, ripens at 2,850 ft.; and it has been observed that the peasant of France, Switzerland, Piedmont, or Savoy, does not fear to carry the hoe to the utmost limit of cultivation. On the N side of St. Bernard the cultivated region reaches 5,530 ft.; on the S side 5,400. On the N side of Mont Cervin it is even pushed up to 6,400 ft.; and on the S side to 6,490 ft. On the N side of Monte Rosa, barley ceases at 5,260 ft.; on the S side, it ascends at certain points to 6,400 ft. Cherry-trees are forced as standards on the Rigi at Unter Dachli, at 3,120 ft.; and the Capuchins of Marie-à-la-Niege occasionally ripen them at 4,290 ft. Walnut-trees disappear at about 2,622 ft. The chestnut does not exist beyond 2,560 ft. On the S side of Monte Rosa, the larch, the alder, and the birch, ascend as high as 7,420 ft. On the N side of the Alps, the fir attains the highest elevation; on the S side, it is the larch. On Ararat, in Armenia, the birch ceases at 8,280 ft.; and on the Caucasus at 7,720 ft. In Lapland, the dwarf-birch ceases to grow at 1,910 ft. Above the region of forests, on the Alps we find the region of stunted pines, Pinus mugho, of rhododendrons, herbaceous willows, alders, and junipers. The region of forests, and that which immediately succeeds, constitute the productive part of the higher Alps: nourishing during summer numerous flocks, which ascend as the snow disappears. The mugho pine ceases at the height of 7,450 ft.; but pasturage extends to 8,530 ft. At this latter elevation we observe the Androsace alpina, A. Helvetica, and A. Pennina, the Silene acaulis, Saxifraga muscoides, S. bryoidii, S. aizoides, and S. stellaris, besides various gentians, alchemillas, and ranunculuses. The higher we ascend, the more do the phænogams diminish in proportion to the cryptogams. On Mont Blanc, Saussure gathered the Silene acaulis at 3,469 metres, or 11,379 ft.; and M. de Welden gathered on Monte Rosa, in the middle of the glacier of Lys, at an elevation of 3,683 metres, stunted Pyrethrum Alpinum, and Phyteuma pauciflorum. At the summit of the Jungfrau, M. Agassiz found, on the gneiss rock in which it terminates, five species of lichen: Lecidea conglomerata, L. confluens, Parmelia elegans, Umbilicaria atropruinosa, and U. virginis. On the chain of the Tropical Andes beautiful phænogamous plants, such as the Calcittum rufescens and Saxifraga Boussingalti, present isolated flowers at elevations of 15,000 ft. [M. Kænitz.] In the subjoined diagram the comparative limits of vegetation on the Alps and Pyrenees are represented. Representing the elevation of Mont Blanc by D; that of Monte Rosa by C; the Pic Nethou in the Pyrenees by B; and Mont Perdu by A; the line h marks the snow-line; E the elevation of the Hospice of 111 E 四 明 m Great St. Bernard; i the elevation of Pinus cembra; k the mean elevation of the glaciers; I the elevation of Briancon, 4,284 ft., and the southern border of the pine zone; m the region of the beech; and n of the oak. The cultivated tracts are represented as running up the sides of the mountains to the region of pines. On the Pyrenean side the extreme limit of the Pinus uncinata is at e; of the Pinus pilea at d; of the Quercus or oak at c; of the chestnut at a, Zoology.] Oxen graze on the Alps at a height of nearly 8,000 ft.; and sheep find pasturage at a still greater elevation. In addition to the immense herds of cattle, sheep, and goats, which are everywhere to be met with in the upper vallies of the Alps, some species of quadrupeds present themselves on the border of the snow-line. The chamois inhabits the upper limit of the forest-region, and the mountain-goat and white hare are to be found in the midst of the glaciers. The next range presents the marmot class; a little lower, those of the bear and mole; and lower still, wolves, foxes, lynxes, and wild cats, are extremely common.—Birds of prey are very numerous. Of these the most formidable are the lammergeyer and eagle, which inhabit the fastnesses of the higher altitudes. The pine forests are frequented by the heath-cock, grouse, water-hens, and partridges. Grebes and other aquatic birds frequent the upland lakes, all of which abound with excellent trout. Of the order of Rapaces 12 species, of Scansores 8, of Oscines 78, of Gallinaceæ 8, of Grallatores 26, and of Natatores 7, or nearly one-half of all the known birds, resident or of passage, in Central Europe, inhabit the lofty regions of St. Bernard, St. Gothard, and the Simplon.—Of reptiles, Rana temporaria occurs here, as in the Pyrenees, in the vicinity of the snow-line; and Rana alpina, at 4,500 ft. The Vipera barus and V. chersea is found at 5,300 ft.; and the Anguis fragilis ascends as high as 6,000 ft.—The insect tribes belonging to the Alpine regions are extremely numerous; but, with the exception of those belonging to the glacial region, present no peculiar species. Passes.] The Alpine chain, formerly deemed all but an insurmountable barrier to the intercommunication of the adjacent countries, is now traversed by numerous passes and several good carriage-roads. The principal of these passes—which it may be well to apprize the reader are always made over the lowest traversable point of a mountain-are: The pass of the Col de Tende, in the Maritime Alps, 5,887 ft. above the level of the sea, the most southern of all the routes across the Alps.—The pass of Mont Genèvre, in the Cottian Alps, executed by order of Napoleon, which connects the vallies of the Durance in France, and the Dora in Piedmont. To the N of this pass is that of the valley of Bardonneche, supposed to be that by which Julius Cæsar crossed the Alps.—The pass of Mont Cenis, in the Graian Alps, formed by Napoleon in 1805, and one of the most frequented of all the great routes which intersect the Alpine chain, is 6,773 ft. above sea-level, 30 m. long, and 18 ft. wide, and leads from the valley of Arc in Savoy, to Turin.-The pass of the Little St. Bernard, which extends nearly a league in length from the valley of the Isère to the Val d'Aosta, and bordered by fine pasture-land, although at an elevation of 7,190 ft. above sea-level, is practicable only for mules. This pass is generally supposed to be that by which Hannibal entered Italy .- The pass of the Great St. Bernard, 7,963 ft. in height, is of historical celebrity as the route by which Napoleon effected the passage of the Alps in 1800.—The pass of St. Cervin, 11,096 ft. above sea-level, between the Great St. Bernard and the Simplon, is the loftiest in Europe.—The pass of the Simplon, another of the beneficial achievements of Napoleon in this stupendous region, formed between 1801 and 1806, which leads from Valais to Milan, is 36 m. in length, and 25 ft. wide throughout. It is nowhere too steep above sea-level. All these three last-enumerated passes are in the Pennine Alps.—The pass of St. Gothard, the chief pass of and now affording a good carriage-road over the Lepontine Alps, is 6,890 ft. in height. It leads from Bellinzona on the S, to Altorf and the lake of Lucerne on the N .- Five good carriage-roads now cross the Rhætian Alps: the first by Monte Bernardino, at 6,700 ft. above sea-level; the second, an old Roman road, crosses the Splugen, and runs from the lake of Como to the Rheinwald; the third, at an alt. of 8,130 ft., runs from Chiavenna to Coire; the fourth, 4,400 ft. above sea-level, connects the vallies of the Inn and Adige, and is the lowest of all the passes of the Alps; and the fifth, 4,660 ft. in height, extends from the valley of the Adige to that of the Sill near Innspruck.—The Noric Alps are crossed, at Radstadler Tauern, by the road which traverses the Carnic range at Tarvis; and by another which leads from Vienna to Trieste and crosses both the Carnic and Julian Alps. Authorities.] De Saussure and Bourrit are the first who examined the system of the Alps with a philosophical eye; and the Voyage dans les Alpes of the former is still our best authority on all that belongs to this department of geographical science. M. Bourrit, in his Description des valées de glace, &c. [1783], led the way to those investigations of the subject of glaciers which have been so ably and profoundly followed out by De Charpentier, Agassiz, Rendu, and our own Forbes, whose Travels through the Alps of Savoy, &c., [Edin. 1843, 8vo.,] is the ablest monograph we yet possess in connexion with the Alpine system.—The following books will all be consulted with advantage on the subject of the geography and natural history of the Alps. König, Reise in den Alpen, 1814.—Necker, Etudes geologiques dans les Alpes. Paris, 1841.—Agassiz, Travail sur les glaciers de la Suisse, 1841.—Bakewell's Travels in 1820-22, 2 vols., 8vo.—Ebel, Manuel du Voyageur en Suisse.

ALPS (DINARIC), a chain which, although generally treated separately, in reality appertains to the vast system of the Alps. It extends from 42° 8' to 45° 30' N lat., and from 14° 40' to 21° 14' E long., through the Austrian states of Croatia, Illyria, and Dalmatia, and the Turkish provinces of Bosnia and Albania. It is connected on the NW with the Julian Alps, and on the SE with the Balkan. The crest of this range separates the rivers which flow into the Danube, by means of the Save, from those which furrow the NE coast of the Adriatic.-This chain may be divided into five parts. The first commences in Illyria with Mount Kleck, where, near the sources of the Kulpa, it detaches itself from the Julian Alps. Its direction is SE, across Military Croatia, and along the boundary separating that territory from Dalmatia. It is composed of two chains nearly parallel, of which the one to the SW under the name of Merslavodicza and Wellebit, ranges the sea coast, while the other bears the names of the Great Kapella, Little Kapella, and Plissivicza. These two chains enclose a considerable plateau, watered by the Lyka and the Gacska, whose waters are lost in the middle of the country.-From the sources of the Titius, or Kerka, to those of the Verbas, a second chain extends from W to E, across Turkish Croatia, under the names of the Chator and the Salliava.—The third chain, or rather third prolongation, reaches from the sources of the Verbas to those of the Bosna. Its direction is SE.—The fourth division also runs SE, from the sources of the Bosna to those of the Drin, and contains Mount Zamora. The fifth follows the same direction as the former, separating Bosnia from the pashalik of Scutari, covering the NE part of that sanjak, and uniting with the Balkan by Mount Perserin to the S of Pristina. for heavy waggons, and its highest point is 6,578 ft. | This last, throughout its great extent, bears the names of Baba-Dagh, Rashka-Bora, and Jamous-Dagh. From its southern flanks descend many tributary torrents of the White Drin, which runs near it in a very deep valley. The Great Kapella runs for 12 leagues from NW to SE, and terminates in the Little Kapella. The Plissivicza runs NNW and SSE, separating the Lyka from Turkish Croatia, for a space of 5 leagues. The branches of the Dinaric Alps are little known. We may, however mention, 1st. Mount Zrin, which separates the basin of the Kulpa from that of the Unna. It is covered with forests. 2d. The steep and wooded branch which, projecting from Mount Chator, forms the dividing line between the basins of the Unna and the Verbas, and bears in its northern part the name of Kosaratz; it also sends off to the NW a branch between the Unna and the Sanna, known by the names of Czerna-Gora, Czervlievitza, Lopata, and Gleermetsch. 3d. The range which, detaching itself from the main chain towards the sources of the Verbas, separates its basin from that of the Bosna, under the names of the Vranja, Radovan, and Vlasich. 4th. The branch which, originating to the SW of Bosna- Serai, separates the basin of the Bosna from that of the Drin. 5th. The branch which commencing to the W of Novibazar, extends to mounts Soubor and Hatibor, between the Western Morava and the Drin, and is divided into two principal branches, one of which runs NW between the Drin and the Kolubarra, and the other E and NE between the basin of the Kolubarra and those of the Morava and the Yessava. From the main chain to the S are detached the four following branches:-1st. The Prologh mountains, which, originating near the springs of the Kerka, run SE along the frontier of Dalmatia and Bosnia, between the Cettina and the Lower Narenta, and project numerous branches which oppose their rugged fronts and sharp points to the Adriatic. 2d. A branch which runs off to the E of Gaska, extends between the basin of the Narenta and those of the lake of Niksiki and the Tribinschitza, and sends-off to the NW a considerable ridge which forces the Narenta to describe a remarkable bend. The 3d are the Montenegro, or Black mountains, which separate the two latter basins from that of the Boyana. 4th. The branch which extends between the White Drin and the Boyana. As this SE portion of the system has not been explored like the other parts of the great range, we know very little either about its elevation or its geological composition. Its NW section bears a great resemblance to the Julian Alps, with which it is immediately connected.
ALPS (SWABIAN). See ALB.
ALPSEE, a small lake of Switzerland, in the cant. of Appenzell, to the SE of the Ebenalp.—Also a small lake of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, SW of Kempten.
ALPSEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, NW of Waldshut.
ALPUENTE, a town and partido of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 25 m. W by N of Jenia. Pop. 1,800.

ALPUJARRAS, a mountain-range of Spain, in Andalusia, an intermediate range between and running parallel to the Sierra Nevada on the N, and the Sierra de Gader on the S, extending from E to W, between the provinces of Granada and Almeria, from Motril to Almeria, a distance of 50 m., with an average breadth of 30 m. Their most rapid descent is towards the S. From their summits, the coast of Barbary and the cities of Tangier and Ceuta may be descried; and they form the last in the successive ranges of sierras which may be descried from the northernmost peak of the rocky ridge of Gibraltar, at a distance of upwards of 100 m. from the point of view. [Capt. C. R. Scott.] The loftiest heights are the Pichacho Mulahacen, 12,762 ft.; the Pichacho de la Valeta, 12,459 ft.; the Cerro de la Alcazaba, 12,300 ft.; the Cerro de los Machos, 12,138 ft.; the Cerro de la Caldera, 10,908 ft.; the Cerro de Tajos altos, 10,890 ft.; and the Picon de Jerez, 10,100 ft. The rivers Olgiva, Almeria, and Almanzor, have their sources in the A. In these mountains silver, copper, and lead were wrought by the Moors, and they still yield the finest alabaster in Europe. The remains of the Moorish pop. of Spain took refuge in this region; and here their descendants have preserved much of the active and industrious spirit of their ancestors. The name is supposed by some to be derived from Alpujar, a celebrated Moorish chief; but Ford says it is a corruption of Al Buxherat, signifying grass, or a pasture-district.
ALQUILQUA, a bay in the strait of Magalhaens, between Cape Upright and Point Echinique, 10 m. to the WNW.
ALQUIZA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 12 m. SSW of Saint Sebastian.

ALRESFORD, a parish in Essex, 5½ m. SE by E of Colchester. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 289.—Also a p. in the hund. of Fawley, in Hampshire. Area 3,660 acres. Pop. 502.—Also a p. and market-town, in the same co., and distinguished from the latter as NEW A., 61 m. NE by E of Winchester. Area of p. 1,250 acres. Pop. 1,578. The town is seated on the Itchin, which is called Alre above it, but takes the name of Itchin below. It has suffered greatly from fire, having been nearly consumed in 1690, in 1710, and in 1736.
ALREWAS, a parish and v. in Staffordshire, 5 m. NE by N of Lichfield. Area 4,350 acres. Pop. 1,173. The v. is 45 m. W of the Walton-on-Trent station of the Birmingham and Derby railway.
ALS, a town of Denmark, on the E coast of Jutland, in the bailiwick of Aalborg, to the N of the Mariager fiord.
ALSA, a stream of Persia, in Azerdbijan, flowing into the Araxes on the r. side.

ALSACE, an obsolete prov. of France, now constituting the modern dep. of the Upper and the Lower Rhine. It was bounded on the N by the palatinate of the Rhine and the archbishopric of Spire, and stretched along the side of the Queiche to its junction with the Rhine which bounded it on the E and separated it from the districts of Ortenau and Brisgau. On the Sit stretched to force of the Danes was stationed on this island, whence ALS the Swiss cantons of Basle and Soleure; on the SW it had the principality of Porentruy and the county of Montbéliard, belonging to the dukes of Wurtemberg; on the W it was coterminous with Lorraine. It was divided into Upper and Lower Alsace by the Eckenbach, a tributary of the Ill; and the southern part of Upper A., from the small river Thure to the Swiss frontier, was distinguished as the Sundgau. This country, which figured largely in the history of the Middle ages, was originally occupied by the Triboques, a German race, whose cap. was called Argontorat. Fredigaire, Latinizing the German name, Elsase or Elsassen, [i. e. 'Dwellers on the Ill,'] called it Elsatia, Elsatium, or Alsatia. Clovis dispossessed the Germans of it; and at his death it formed a province of the kingdom of Metz. By the treaty of Verdun it was included in the kingdom of Lorraine; and it subsequently became part of the German empire, and was given to the dukes of Swabia-styled dukes of Swabia and Alsace-who committed the civil administration of A. to two provincial counts who took the title of landgraves, and upon the extinction of the house of Swabia became masters of the province. In 1648 a considerable part of A., then incorporated with the empire, was ceded by Austria to France. In 1673 Louis took possession of the principal fortified places in A., viz., Haguenau, Colmar, and Schelestadt, and ultimately, by the treaty of Ryswick, added the city of Strasburg, and the whole of the prov. to his dominions. The ecclesiastical government continued as before under the bishops of Basle and Spire, the archb. of Besançon, and the bishops of Strasburg and Metz; but the civil and military government underwent considerable alterations. A governor-general was appointed with a commanderin-chief, two lieutenant generals, and other officials under him; and the government of Strasburg was not included in the general government until 1776. As to the civil government it was intrusted to the sovereign council of Alsace at Colmar, which exercised the same rights and privileges as the parliament of the kingdom. At this time Lower Alsace, with the territory of Strasburg, comprehended five districts or subdelegations: viz., the lordship of Liechtenberg being the most extensive in A., which was divided into nine bailiwicks and held by the landgrave of Hesse-Darm stadt; the subdelegation of Landau, including the bailiwicks of Lauterburg and Gutemberg; the subdelegation of Wissemburg; that of Saverne including the bailiwick of Haguenau; and that of Schelestadt. Upper Alsace, which under the domination of Austria had Ensisheim for its capital, comprehended two districts, Colmar, and Belfort the chief town of the Sundgau, with the lordships of Ferrette and Altkirch, divisions of the ancient county of Ferrette, and the royal city of Huningue. In 1790 the province of Alsace was formed into the departments of the Upper and Lower Rhine. In 1815 the principality of Porentruy was ceded to Berne; and that of Montbéliard was annexed to the dep. of Doubs. By the treaty of Paris, a considerable portion of the dep. of the Lower Rhine was ceded to Bavaria.
ALSACE, a township in Berks, co. Pennsylvania, U. S., on the E side of the Schuylkill. Pop. 2,498.
ALSADAMUM (Mount), a mountain of Syria, in Jebel Haouran, in N lat. 32° 40'. It is called by the natives Kelb Haouran.
ALSA'LIH, a small town near the S coast of Arabia, 10 m. NE of Ras Seilan. It contains 200 houses, and a pop. of about 500.—Haines.
ALSASUA, a town of Spain, in Navarre, 30 m. WNW of Pampeluna.
ALSBIERG, a town of Denmark, in N Jutland, 21 m. W of Aalborg, on the Lym river, in N lat. 57° 2'.
ALSCHANKA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Saratov, 60 m. NW of Atkarsk.
ALSEMBERG, a town of Belgium, in S Brabant, 6 m. S of Brussels. Pop. 740.

ALSEN, an island of Denmark, in the Little Belt, off the east coast of Schleswig; central lat. 54° 58' N. It is 20 m. in length from NW to SE; and from 3 to 9 m. in breadth, and of very irregular outline. It is separated from the main by a very narrow sound which may be crossed in a few minutes but is deep enough to float the largest ships; and from the island of Funen on the NE and E by a channel varying in breadth from 10 to 15 m. The pop. amounts to about 15,000, of whom 2,500 are collected in the cap. Sonderborg on the SW coast. Its soil is good, producing grain, fruit, potatoes, and flax; and the pop. is chiefly supported by agriculture and gardening. The ducal family of Sonderborg held the southern part of this island till 1667, when it was made over to the Crown. The Norburg, or northern district, belonged to the house of Plön, but was likewise ceded to the Crown in 1730. During the war between Denmark and the duchies of Schleswig and Holstein in 1848, the main they kept up communication with their intrenchments ! on the main, on the Dubbeler heights opposite Sonderborg.

ALSENS, or Alsenz, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Rhine, on the l. bank of a stream of the same name which falls into the Nahe 6 m. below the town. Pop. 1,454. Coal is wrought in the neighbourhood.
ALSFELD, a town of Hessen, on the Schwalm, 32 m. NNE of Giessen. Pop. 3,800.
ALSH (Loch), a narrow and irregularly shaped arm of the sea, stretching between the SE point of Skye and the mainland of Ross-shire, and penetrating into the district of Kintail, in N lat. 57° 15'.
ALSHEIM, a town of Hessen, in the prov. of the Rhine, 5 m. S by W of Oppenheim. Pop. 1,600.

ALSLEBEN, a town of Prussian Saxony, on the 1. bank of the Saal, 9 m. SW of Bernberg. Pop. in 1837, 1,731.—Also two towns in Anhalt Dessau, though environed by Prussian territory, between Magdeburg and Halbertstadt, one of which, distinguished as Great A., has a pop. of 1,000; the other, and smaller town, is within a little distance. Both are chiefly inhabited by linen-weavers.
ALSTAHOUG, a town of Norway, on the island of Alsten; the seat of the bishop of Nordland and Finmark.
ALSTEAD, a township of Cheshire co., New Hampshire, U. S., 14 m. N of Keene. Pop. 1,454.

ALSTEN, or ALSTENAE, an island of Norway, in N lat. 65° 44′, E long. 11° 50′. It is a small rugged mountainous island, in which the peaks of the Syv- System, or Seven Sisters, attain an alt. of 4,380 ft.

ALSTER, a river of Germany, rising in two streams in the district of Stormar in Holstein, and flowing into the r. bank of the Elbe at Hamburg after a course of about 30 m. It skirts the W side of the city, and forms on the N side two fine basins called the Inner and the Outer A. It also supplies various canals intersecting the city.
ALSTOED, a town of Denmark, in Zealand, 5 m. S of Soroe, in N lat. 55° 24'.
ALSTON. See ALDSTONE.
ALSVIG, an island of the Hebrides, on the NW coast of Skye, about 2 m. in circuit.
ALT, a river of Lancashire, which runs into the Irish channel, at Altmouth, 8 m. WSW of Ormskirk. -Also a v. in the cant. of St. Gall, Switzerland, 13 m. NE of Appenzell.
ALT VATER, a mountain of Silesia, on the borders of Moravia, rising to an alt. of 4,400 ft.
ALTA, a town of Peru, 60 m. NW of Arequipa.— Also a river of Russia, in the gov. of Poltava.

ALTA GRACIA, a town of Guayana, on the r. bank of the Orinocco, in N lat. 7° 40', W long. 66° 6'. -Also a v. of Venezuela, on the E coast of the lake of Maracaybo, near its entrance.—Also a town of La Plata, on the Segundo river, 30 m. S of Cordova.

ALTAI, an extensive mountain-system in Central Asia, which extends from 43° to 52° N lat., and from 87° to 97° E long. of Greenwich. But the term has been used by some geographers in a far more extensive application than that now indicated; giving it more than 100 degrees of longitudinal extent, and inducing the idea that a continuous uninterrupted range of lofty mountains forms the southern boundary of Western as well as Eastern Siberia, than which nothing can be more remote from truth. Erman too has shown that a totally different geological formation commences at the S extremity of the Baikal lake, and characterises the mountain-ranges of Kingan to Jablonoi Krebet, in which Pallas and others trace the prolongation of the Altai to the shores of the sea of Okotsk. Humboldt also denies the existence of any mountain-chain connecting the Altai ALT and the Ural on the other side of the continent, as the Krasnoyarsk chain is by some represented to do. In fact, the name Altai should be restricted to the great range which, running from SW to NE, separates Zungaria from Mongolia; and which, in ancient times, formed a great political as well as physical boundary in the northern part of the great central plateau of the Asiatic continent. The most prominent masses of the system thus defined are found between the parallels of 49° and 51° N. Though the Altai range, thus defined, is famous in the traditionary accounts of the Mongols, it is but very imperfectly known: little has yet been determined respecting its extreme height, or the limit of vegetation upon it, or that of perpetual snow. The Chinese-by no means very accurate geographers—usually comprehend under the name Altai, the chains which extend to the SW of Central Asia, towards the boundary of the two Bokharas, between the sources of the Kashghar river, the Sirr, the Jihun, the Ili, and the Tekes rivers. According to the nomadic tribes, the Altai is the northern prolongation of the Bogdo-Ula, or Bokhdaula, a name which Pallas gives to the whole chain of the Teenshan or Tianshan; but a deep depression is known to exist between these ranges, in which is comprehended the basin of the Ili. The Kalmucks, who inhabit the west bank of the Irtish, do not consider the mountain-ridges of their cantons as a part of the Altai, but as western branches of the Zungarian mountains, known to them under the general name of Tagh or Tau. The Saratau forms, in some measure, the border of the highest of the Altaian summits towards the west. It lies to the NE, at a small distance from the entrance of the Irtish into the Saisang or Dsaisang lake; and joins the Tarbagatai on the south. Its highest summit, to the east of the sources of the Irtish, is the Savra, which is covered with snow at the beginning of July. The Saratau is clothed with larches in the lower half of its height; but the Altai proper is entirely bare. From the meridian of Oost-Kamenogorsk the Altai system is prolonged towards the W under the parallel of 49° or 50° over an extent of 160 leagues. Its highest part is towards the sources of the Irtish and Obi, allowing the Shabekan, the most SE branch and commencement of the latter, to pass through a narrow gorge. On the N, a valley comes between it and the Metallic mountains of Kolyvan; on the E it joins the Tangnu mountains. It comprehends all the country from the Irtish towards the Baikal lake, and the Angara. This range forms three ramifications more or less high, whose plateaus are partly fertile and cultivated.—1st. The mountains of Kolyvan, between the Irtish and the Ob. These decline northward to the level of the steppe of Baraba, and are rich in gold, silver, copper, lead, and zinc.—2d. The mountains of Kutsnetz, or Kusnetzk, running from SSE to NNW, and striking off from the Altai under the meridian of 87° 20' E, between the Ob and the Jenisei. Of these, the Sabina-Dabau is the highest.—3d. The mountains of Sayan or Sayansk, which prolong themselves from the banks of the Jenisei to those of the Lena and to the Baikal lake. These three subdivisions are sometimes known by the common name of the Ore mountains of the Altai. At its eastern extremity towards the Angara this range joins the mountains of Tangnu, from the northern declivities of which the numerous head-streams of the Jenisei descend northwards. The parts of the Little Altai best known to the Russians, consist of nine subdivisions.—1st. The Kolyvanskoi mountains.—2d. The Korbolinskoi.—3d. The Alaskaia.—4th. The Oubinskoi.—5th. The Buktarminskoi.-6th. The Teletskoi.-7th.-The Tsharyshkoi. -8th. The Kutsnitzkoi.-9th. The Krasnoyarskoi. Most of these chains are denominated from the streams which water the vallies, and furrow the circumjacent ridges, as the Alay, the Korbola, the Uba, the Buktarma, and the Tsharysh. The Kolyvan branch, the NW extremity of the Altai, is about 70 m. in extent from SE to NW; and its highest summits do not much exceed 3,000 ft. The town of that name is 1,290 ft. above the sea on the right bank of the Bielaya, near its junction with the Ob. The Schlangenberg, or 'Mountain of serpents,' called Smejewskeja Gora by the Russians, formerly the richest silver mining-station in Kolyvan, at the NW extremity of this range, 280 versts SW of Barnaoul, has an alt. of 1,282 ft. above the sea. In its vicinity many mountains rear their heads, amongst which is the Karaulnaia-Sopka or Wachtberg 2,160 ft. above the sea. The Sinnaia-Sopka, the most advanced NW promontory towards the Kolyvan lake, rises 4,416 ft. above its base. It is joined to the snowy summits by a high plateau, and cut by deep ravines.—The Snowy Altai ranges extend from 50° to 52° N lat. between the Khatunya and the Irtish, and from 82° 20' to 85° 20' E long. They differ from all the other mountains in their aspect, as they do not present, like them, insulated and sharp peaks. The Bielki of the Ulba, the Uba, the Tighirek, the Korgon, the Anoui, and the Bachalak, are branches of the Snowy range. Between the snowy summits of these mountains are broad and steep vallies, without any vegetation save lichens and dwarf-birches; but affording pasture in summer to elks, stags, rein-deer, and chamois. The most common quadruped in these regions is the Lagomys Alpinus, which herd together in summer, and shelter themselves in winter in the clefts of the rocks. Three points of the Bielki of Tighirek have been barometrically determined, viz., the highest summit 940 toises, or 6,016 ft.; the mine of Beryls, 5,880 ft., and the dwelling of the miners, 4,855 ft. The whole of the Bielki, as well as the more northern parts of what is generally known as the Little Altai, were traversed in 1826 by M. Ledebuhr, a professor of botany at Dorpat. He ascended the summit of the Revennaia-Sopka, or 'Mountain of rhubarb,' between the Schlangenberg and Kolyvan, alt. 4,266 ft.; and made an excursion to the mine of Riddersk, 184 versts from Schlangenberg, crossing in his way the crest of the range which separates the basin of the Irtish from that of the Ob. This mine is in the Bielki of the Ulba, and produces silver and lead; it was discovered in 1782, and is elevated 2,502 ft. above sea-level. The valley of Riddersk is watered by the Grammatowka or Great Ulba, and the rocks are covered with birches and Siberian pines. The Kreutzberg, which he ascended, is the loftiest summit of the Bielki of Ulba, and elevated 7,073 ft. above the sea.—The mountain plateau of the Koksun, or Kok-ous-sun, is 6,968 ft. in height. This plateau makes part of the intermediate chain separating the basin of the Irtish from that of the Ob, and runs N and S, finally joining at its southern extremity the Alps or high mountains of Tighiretskoi or Tigerœzki, whence flow the Tsharysh, the Korgon, and other rivers of Siberia. Towards the W the Koksun chain forms the mountains of Ubinsk and Riddersk, and these latter in their turn join the mountains of Turgusunsk, which farther S are called the Kholzun range. In this concatenation of mountains are recognised two parallel ranges running E and W. The northern of these ranges is traversed by the basins of the Tsharysh and the Khatunya. The Koksun mountains present a complete desert, and are strewed with the debris of decomposed rocks. From the summit Ledebuhr had a beautiful view over the neighbouring chain, on which the sunny summits of dazzling white contrasted finely with the obscurity of the deep vallies and the verdure of the inferior slopes. On the eastern side of this range rise the sources of the Koksun, one of which he found at an elevation of 6,072 ft., surrounded by large trunks of the Pinus cembro; the last birches appeared at an elevation of 5,614 ft. above the sea. The Little Koksun is the most western source of the Khatunya, or western branch of the Obi, which conducts to the Icy sea all the waters of these mountains. This small stream unites with the Great Koksun, and takes, at its confluence with the Uimon, the name of Khatunya, or Katunia. The Little Koksun runs in a frightful ravine, without a track alongst its banks save that made by the steps of the elk, the stag, or the antelope. At the elevation of 6,350 ft., springs the Uluchei, a tributary of the Great Koksun; and on the NW side rises the Tsharysh. To the NE of the source of the Uluchei, Ledebuhr ascended a mountain apparently the most elevated of all those around, and very steep, being 7,663 ft. above the sea. Here a trunk of the Pinus cembro was found growing at the elevation of 6,975 ft., and birches at 7,191 ft. Many scarce and curious plants are found in the romantic valley of the Tsharysh, where he encamped at an elevation of 3,864 ft. The village of Uimon, below the confluence of the Koksun and the Uimon-forming the Khatunya—is 3,354 ft. above the sea. The highest summit of the Altai that has yet been measured is in the Alps of Tighiretskoi, the Ilatilzkoikanun, 10,735 ft. above the sea. The Rossyponykanun, 6,188 ft. high, is famous for its numerous cavities, in which are found great masses of beryl and other coloured crystals. At the foot of the Sinnaia-Sopka appears the Bieloi-Osero, or 'White lake,' a beautiful sheet of water, with an island in the middle. Towards the Tsharysh and Tom are immense rocks of porphyry, with jasper, and rolled debris of rocks, covering the inferior and middle plateaus of the mountains. There are found in many places, and especially along the Tom and Upper Inia, rocks announcing a volcanic origin, and petrified trees in alluvial sand. It would appear, from the observations of Renovantz, Herman, Laxman, Pallas, Patrin, and others, that the geognostic relations of this vast range of mountain groups correspond with those of other Alpine countries. The centre and highest parts of the groups are in general composed of the first or oldest granite formation, on which rest, with lower and lower levels corresponding to the age of the formation, gneiss, mica slate, and clay slate. Along with these rocks, beds of trap, serpentine, and limestone, are observed; and the whole series are frequently covered with the second porphyry, (the porphyry contains jasper,) second serpentine, and sienite formations. We have not met with any account of the occurrence of transition rocks, although there is not a doubt, that careful examination will discover them in this great range. Mineralogists mention chalcedony, cornelian, &c., as occurring in different parts of the range; it is probable that they belong either to the transition or fleetz trap. The lower parts of the range are in many places composed of fleetz rocks, as sandstone conglomerate, sandstone, limestone, gypsum, marl, trap, &c., but the formations have not been ascertained. The lowest parts, as the vallies and plains, are covered with alluvial rocks of different kinds. Metalliferous repositories, containing gold, silver, copper, lead, zinc, and iron, occur in different places, and in some in great abundance. Obscure and broken legends of the riches of the Kin-shan or Golden mountain, as the Altai is called by the Chinese, tempted Peter the Great to frequent but unprofitable military expeditions to the Upper Irtish. In 1723, the success of his searches for copper ore, induced Nikitas Demidoff, a son of the celebrated miner of the Ural, to undertake a closer examination of the Altai ranges, and in 1736 the veins of the Schlangenberg mine, rich in gold and silver, were fully opened. This mine is the property of the Crown. In 1827, it yielded 1,000 poods [40,000 lbs.] of silver in mass; 916 poods of pure silver; and 27 poods 26 pounds of gold. The total value of the gold and silver yielded annually by the mines of the A. is about £190,000 sterling, of which nearly 75 per cent. is clear profit. They also yield about 220 tons of copper, and 360 of lead .- The only known hot springs of the A. are near the sources of the Berel, in the valley of Rachmanowka. They rise in crystalline slate rock.—Humboldt, Fragmens Asiatiques, 1833, and Asie Centrale, 1843 .- Ritter's Erkunde.—Klaproth's Tableau Historique de l'Asie.-Reise nach dem Ural, dem Altai, &c., von Humboldt, Ehrenberg, und Rose.
ALTAMAHA. See ALATAMAHA.

ALTAMIRA, a town of Spain, in Gallicia, on the Tamara river, 15 m. W of San Jago de Compostella. -Also a town of Mexico, in the district of Potosi, on the N bank of a lake near the mouth of the Panuco or Tampico river, 18 m. NW of Tampico. It consists of a few houses and a collection of thatched cottages, overlooking a desolate prospect of swamps and lagunes, and of course very unhealthy.—Also a town of Venezuela, near the source of the Chivata, a river running S to the Orinocco, 50 m. NE by N of Alta Gracia.
ALTAMONTE, a town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, 15 m. NW of Bisagnano. Pop. 3,000.

ALTAMURA, a town and cant. of Naples, in the Terra di Barri, 26 m. SW of Bari, in N lat. 40° 47'. Pop. 14,949. It lies at the southern foot of Monte Lupolo, is the seat of a bishopric, and has the title of a principality.

ALTARE, a town of Sardinia, in the territory of Genoa, on the border of Piedmont, in N lat. 44° 21', E long. 8° 20'. It lies in the central range of the Ligurian Apennines, at an elevation of 1,368 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,100.
ALTARNUN, a parish of Cornwall, in Lesnewth hund. Area 13,840 acres. Pop. 1,334.
ALTAVELA, a rocky islet off the S coast of Hayti, in N lat. 17° 28', W long. 71° 40'. It rises to an alt. of 500 ft., and is 61 m. from the SW point of Beata.
ALTAVILLA, a town of Naples, in Principato Citra, in the district and 8 m. S of Campagna. Pop. 3,684.—Also a town in the prov. of Principato Ultra, 9 m. N of Avellino. Pop. 2,956.
ALTBETSCHE, a town of Hungary, on the l. bank of the Theiss, to the N of the Batsches canal.
ALTBURG, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of Schwarzwald, in N lat. 48° 44'. Pop. 1,500.
ALTCAR, a parish of Lancashire, on the Alt, <math>6\frac{1}{2}</math> m. W by S of Ormskirk. Area 3,580 acres. Pop. 490.

ALTDORF, or ALTORF, a town of Switzerland, the cap. of the cant. of Uri, near the junction of the Staubi with the Reuss, 20 m. SE of Lucerne, at an elevation of 1,631 ft. above sea-level, in N lat. 46° 55', E long. 8° 37½. Pop. 1,650. It is on the high road to Italy by Mount St. Gothard; but is a place of little trade, and appears never to have recovered from its desolation by fire in 1799. Its principal attraction is its association with the memory of the patriot Tell. Tradition relates that he was born in the little v. of Burglen, close by; and that it was in the square in the centre of A. that he shot off the apple from the head of his child, when the tyrant Geisler subjected him to the inhuman trial.—Also a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Rezat, on the Schwarzach, 12 m. ESE of Nuremberg. Pop. 2,120. A university, founded here in 1570, was united to that of Erlangen in 1809. Coal and zinc are wrought in the neighbourhood.— Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Bodensee, on the Schussen, 5 m. NE of Ravensburg. ALT Pop. 2,120.—Also a town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine. Pop. 1,391; and a v. in the bailiwick of Engen, in the circle of the Lake. Pop. 389. -Also a town of Hungary, in the district of Zips, near the r. bank of the Dunajec.

ALTEA, a town and partido of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 27 m. NE of Alicant, on an eminence three-quarters of a mile from the coast. Pop. 4,500. Cotton, lint, silk, honey, and wine, are produced in the surrounding country.
ALTEFAHR, a town of Prussia, on the W coast of the isle of Rugen, 12 m. WSW of Bergen. It was at one time strongly fortified, for the defence of the strait which separates Rugen from the main.

ALTE LAND, an administrative division of Hanover, in the landrost of Stade, on the l. bank of the Elbe. Area 45,000 acres. Pop. 15,693. Its soil is fertile though marshy; and it is watered by the Schwinge, the Este, and the Lube. As a part of the ancient duchy of Bremen, the inhabitants enjoy peculiar laws and privileges. The cap. is York. ALTEN, ALTENAA, or ALTENELF, a river of Norway, in Finmark, flowing into the Altenfiord, after a course of 80 m. in a N direction, in which it separates Nordland and Finmark, and passes Altengaard, or Alten, in N lat. 69° 54'. The mean height of the barom. at A. from Oct. 1837 to Sept. 1838, was 29.771 inches; the mean temp. 32°.

ALTENA, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, district of Arnsberg, on the Leine and Nette rivers, 30 m. NNE of Cologne. Pop. of the town in 1837, 4,295; of the circle in 1831, 34,172, of whom 724 were Catholics. The principal manufacture of the town is that of needles, thimbles, buckles, and small iron wares.—Also a district and v. of the Netherlands, in S. Holland, between the Maes and the Biesboch, 10 m. SW of Gorcum.
ALTENAHR, a town of Prussia, in the district of Coblentz, 3 m. WSW of Ahrweiler, on the Ahr. Pop. 346.

ALTENAU, a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Grubenhagen, in the Harz district, 4 m. E of Klausthal, at an elev. of 1,455 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,620. Silver, lead, iron, and copper, are mined in the vicinity of this town.—Also a small river of Brunswick, which, rising near Badeleben, flows from E to W into the Ocker.
ALTENBECKEN, a village of Prussia, in W stphalia, 9 m. ENE of Paderborn. Pop. 700.

ALTENBERGA, a small mining town and district of Saxony, in the Erzgeberge, near the sources of the Muglitz, at an alt. of 2,559 ft. Pop. of the town in 1801, 1,374; in 1837, 2,036. Iron, alum, and tin, are extensively wrought in the environs. The mean temp. of A. is 40° 40′; extreme of cold 21°; of heat 82°.
ALTENBREITUNGEN, a village of Saxe-Meiningen, 15 m. NNW of Meiningen. Pop. 800.
ALTENBRUCH, a village of Hanover, in the landrost of Stade, 6 m. W of Ortemdorf, on the l. bank of the Elbe, and at its embouchure. Pop. 2,500.

ALTENBURG, or SAXE-ALTENBURG, a duchy of Saxony, consisting of two portions,—the eastern lying on the Pleisse river, and the western on the Saale, the principality of Gera or Reuss interposing betwixt them. The eastern part is bounded on the NW by Prussian Saxony; on the NE and E by the kingdom of Saxony; on the SW by Weimar; and on the W by Reuss. The western has Prussian Saxony on the N; Reuss on the E; Weimar on the SE; and Coburg and Schwarzburg on the S and SW. The area of the eastern division or circle is 11.5 German, or 247.25 English sq. m., and the pop. in 1846 was 82,816. The area of the western division or circle of Saal- Eisenberg is 12.5 German, or 268.75 English sq. m., with a pop. in 1846 of 46,000. The total area of the duchy, therefore, is 24 German, or 566 English sq. m.—The surface of both divisions is mountainous, or hilly rather; but less so in the eastern than in the western. The most elevated summits, viz., the Dolenstein and the Buchberg, do not exceed 1,000 ft. The rivers are all tributaries of the Elbe. Those of the eastern circle are the Pleisse; of the western, the Saale, with its tributaries the Orla and Roda. The climate is mild, and the soil fertile, producing good crops of rye and wheat, besides potatoes, pulse, flax, hemp, and rape-seed. There is little mining; and manufacturing industry is nearly confined to coarse woollens, stockings, and wooden ware. - The pop. is chiefly Protestant of the Lutheran confession. The Catholics do not exceed 300.—On the extinction of the house of A. in 1672, the larger portion of this principality became vested in the family of Saxe- Gotha, which expired in 1825, when the duke of Saxe-Hildburghausen resigned his duchy to the duke of Meiningen, receiving in lieu of it the duchy of A., with the exception of some small districts. In 1831, the late duke granted a constitution to his subjects, according to which 12 delegates, who are elected every five years, form a legislative chamber, under a president nominated by the duke. The estimated revenue of this little state for four years, from 1845 to 1848, is 631,940 thalers. The debt in 1848 was 930,687 thalers. The military force consists of four companies of infantry. The contingent to the army of the confederation is 992 men.—The principal towns are Altenburg, the cap., Schmöllen on the Sprotta, Gössnitz on the Pleisse, Meuselwitz on the Schnauder, Ronneburg, Eisenberg, Roda, Orlamunde, and Sahla or Kahla. The town of ALTENBURG, the cap. of the above, once an imperial city and the cap. of the duchy of Pleissen, is situated in N lat. 55° 50', near the l. bank of the Pleisse, 16 m. S of Leipzig, on the Saxo-Bavarian line of railway, which, running up the valley of the Pleisse, skirts the town on the N and E sides, passing between it and the river. It is a well-built town, and conducts a considerable trade in cattle, timber, and corn. Its manufactures are linen, ribands, gloves, sealingwax, pottery, and tobacco. Pop. in 1822, 10,604; in 1842, 14,500.
